Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»Tatjana Smith in Paris: “I had no idea where I was, literally most of the race”

What a moment.

Tatjana Smith couldn’t hide her sheer delight after claiming gold in the 100m breaststroke event at the Paris 2024 Olympics.

“I had no idea where I was, literally most of the race,” admitted Smith afterwards.

“I actually didn’t think I medalled because when I turned to my left, the two blocks next to me both had lights on and it felt like someone else on the other side touched first.

“So I actually didn’t think I was medalling.

“My main goal was just to not do what I did in Tokyo and look around, so literally the last 15 metres, I was like, no, close your eyes, let’s just go.”

“I swam for everyone that’s been there for me, that’s believed with me, for our country as well.”

Tatjana Smith was the resounding pre-race favourite for the 100m breaststroke event after winning her heat and semi-finals. She broke the world record to claim 200m gold in Tokyo three years ago and now she’s climbed to the top of the mountain in the shorter race. She touched the wall first in1.05.28despite a less-than-perfect first lap and thanks to a stunning surge in the final 25. China’s Tang Qianting scooped silver and a visibly delighted Mona McSharry, from Ireland, got the bronze.

Smith stands to become only the second swimmer in history after Penny Heyns in 1996 to complete the 100m and 200m breaststroke golden double at the same Olympics event. Smith wasn’t yet born when Heyns accomplished this historic feat. The 27-year-old contests her 200m breaststroke heat onWednesday 31 July at 11:00 SA timewith thesemi-finals going at 21:51 and 21:58.The finalis onThursday 01 August at 21:04.You know thatNando’s will be watching. Smith won this event in Tokyo with a time of 2.18.95, a world record at the time.

You’ve seen it hundreds of times already but what’s the harm in watching it a few more times? One of the great comebacks in Olympic swimming history.
